title: The Anthropic Founders Changed How I Use Claude
summary: Austin Marchese presents four rules for deciding what to build with Claude, distilled from public statements by Anthropic founders Dario and Daniela Amodei. The video offers guidance for individuals and businesses applying large language models to side projects, startups, and operational tasks.
writeup: Drawing on recent public remarks by Dario and Daniela Anthropic at the Code with Claude conference and Stanford, this video extracts four decision rules Anthropic uses to choose what to build. The central argument is that AI collapses three historic barriers to shipping (cost, domain expertise, and time) and that the same framework governing a frontier lab scales directly to an individual side project. Each rule comes with a concrete heuristic the presenter has stress-tested against his own client work.
key_points: Anthropic has recalibrated three barriers that previously killed project ideas: cost (Dario states software may become 'essentially free,' removing the need to amortize across millions of users), domain expertise (Daniela notes Claude lets non-engineers build websites with a few clicks), and time (Claude Code was built in a week and a half almost entirely with Claude Opus 4.8, per Dario).

The verification filter blocks release whenever the cost of error is high: Anthropic withheld its Claude Mythos model because it could not yet prove consistent reliability, and the same logic caused a client email-drafting automation to fail because outputs could not be verified before deployment.

Boris Cherny, creator of Claude Code, stated that giving Claude a feedback loop to verify its own work produces a 2 to 3x improvement in final output quality, making self-verification a core build discipline rather than an afterthought.

Anthropic explicitly defines both an ICP (developers are the most important users, per Daniela) and an audience anti-goal (no image or video generation, per Dario), and this focus is credited with Anthropic growing 80x in Q1 2026 against a 10x plan while OpenAI pursued general consumers, image generation, video, enterprise, and social media simultaneously.

The 'middle to middle' framing (rule four) holds that humans should own the start (framing the problem) and the end (delivering the final product) while AI works the middle, contrasting with 'end to end' prompting where the user walks away and hopes for a usable result.

Rule 4: Build Middle to Middle, Not End to End
